criteria_results: Found: yes, won, posthumous, best supporting actor, 2009
results.actual_response: Yes, Heath Ledger won an Oscar for Best Supporting Actor for his iconic portrayal of the Joker in "The Dark Knight." The award was posthumously awarded to him at the 81st Academy Awards in 2009. Ledger's performance as the Joker was widely praised by critics and audiences alike, and his win was a significant recognition of his talent and dedication to the role.

"The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King" won 11 Oscars at the 76th Academy Awards in 2004. The awards it received include Best Picture, Best Director for Peter Jackson, Best Adapted Screenplay, Best Original Score, Best Original Song, Best Visual Effects, Best Art Direction, Best Costume Design, Best Makeup, Best Sound Mixing, and Best Film Editing.

Sources:

User: When did the first Avatar movie premiere in theaters?

criteria_results: Found: new hope, original title, episode iv
results.actual_response:
The original title of the first Star Wars movie released in 1977 was "Star Wars." It was later renamed "Star Wars: Episode IV - A New Hope" to fit within the larger Star Wars saga after the subsequent release of sequels. This change occurred in 1981 when the movie was re-released in theaters. The director and creator of the Star Wars franchise is George Lucas. 

Sources: 
- IMDb: https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0076759/
- ScreenRant: https://screenrant.com/star-wars-movie-titles-changes-explained/

results.actual_response: "Pulp Fiction" was released in 1994 and was nominated for seven Academy Awards in 1995, including Best Picture. However, it did not win the Best Picture Oscar. The award for Best Picture in 1995 went to "Forrest Gump," directed by Robert Zemeckis. "Pulp Fiction" did win the Oscar for Best Original Screenplay for Quentin Tarantino and Roger Avary.

User: Did Pulp Fiction win the Best Picture Oscar in 1995?

prompt_text:
You are CineMind, a movie analysis agent. Domain: films, filmmakers, actors, genres, box office, awards, trivia, comparisons, recommendations. Stay film-focused only.

DATA: Prioritize real-time searches (IMDb, RT, Wikipedia, Variety/Deadline, YouTube). Label info as CONFIRMED/RUMOR/SPECULATION. State confidence for upcoming releases/casting/box office.

SPOILERS: Default = no spoilers (themes/setup only). If user requests spoilers, start with "⚠️ SPOILER WARNING: [Film Title]" and explain significance.

RECOMMENDATIONS: Reference user preferences → explain connections (themes/genres/directors/actors) → provide variety (genres/eras/countries, known + hidden gems) → be specific about similarities and uniqueness.

STYLE: Enthusiastic but professional. Clear structure. Cite sources naturally. Adapt complexity to query.

UNCERTAINTY: Distinguish "not available" vs "don't know", "sources conflict" vs "unsure". Offer to search further.

RESPONSES: Lead with current info → cite throughout → distinguish facts/rumors/speculation → organize clearly → add context → offer follow-up.
